metadata.title: Build a Greenwashing Claims Risk Checker with Claude
metadata.note: cli-ideas Card 22 — Ch 15 (Brand Ethics, Purpose, and Sustainability). DOUBLE-CHECK: EU ECGT Directive adopted March 2024; member states transpose by 27 March 2026; rules apply from 27 September 2026. Two specific bans: (1) generic green claims without substantiation ('eco-friendly', 'green', 'sustainable', 'environmentally responsible', 'nature-friendly', 'climate-smart'); (2) product-level 'climate neutral' / 'net zero' based primarily on carbon offsets rather than actual value-chain emissions reductions. Green Claims Directive (GCD): withdrawn by European Commission June 2025 — NOT operative. FTC Green Guides: last revised 2012, under review since 2022, no updated version as of early 2026. Status tags per claim: HAVE / NEED / CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE. Risk: HIGH-REGULATORY / MEDIUM / LOW. Flag: [ECGT-RISK]. Nordhaus DICE model: reduction-vs-offset distinction underpins the offset ban.

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — Claude UI, ask shown answered; IN-FOR-BEAR
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Build it with Claude, then take it apart.

props.command: claude "run greenwashing risk check on this brand's sustainability claims — ECGT and FTC flags, status per claim"
output: 'eco-friendly packaging' → [ECGT-RISK] HIGH-REGULATORY — generic green, no substantiation
CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE: no certification or documented methodology present
'carbon neutral by 2030 via offsets' → [ECGT-RISK] HIGH-REGULATORY — offset-based climate neutral banned
Revised: 'reducing packaging weight 30% by 2027 — see methodology at [URL]'

beats.role_note: stakes — BEFORE CLI loop; SHOW-DON'T-TELL: ECGT timeline with claims banned and fine exposure
beats.narration_text: In March 2024, the EU adopted the Empowering Consumers for the Green Transition Directive — ECGT. Member states were required to transpose it into national law by 27 March 2026. The rules apply from 27 September 2026. ECGT bans two specific claim categories. First: generic green claims without substantiation. The words eco-friendly, green, sustainable, environmentally responsible, nature-friendly, and climate-smart are banned at the product level unless accompanied by recognized substantiation — a certification, a documented methodology, a consumer-accessible source. Not a tone-of-voice update. A legal requirement. Second: product-level climate neutral or net zero claims based primarily on carbon offsets rather than actual value-chain emissions reductions. The Green Claims Directive — a more detailed standalone framework — was withdrawn by the European Commission in June 2025. ECGT is the operative rule. The FTC Green Guides, last revised in 2012 and under review since 2022, govern the US market — no updated version as of 2026. A claim that ships today as brand copy may be a legal violation by autumn. The checker finds it now.
beats.visual_intent: Timeline: March 2024 ECGT adopted → 27 March 2026 member states transpose → 27 September 2026 rules apply; two banned categories listed as red boxes: (1) generic green claims + list of banned words, (2) offset-based climate neutral; Green Claims Directive withdrawn June 2025 shown as struck-through; FTC Green Guides 2012 shown in amber (under review); no composer yet

beats.role_note: ClaudeComposerAsk — show and discuss the prompt
beats.narration_text: Into Claude. Given a list of sustainability or ethics claims, run a greenwashing risk check against two standards: EU ECGT and FTC Green Guides. For each claim: rate the risk as HIGH-REGULATORY — a specific banned category under ECGT or presumptively deceptive under FTC; MEDIUM — claims where substantiation is required but type of evidence is ambiguous; or LOW — specific, verifiable claims with accessible evidence. Assign a status: HAVE, the substantiation exists; NEED, the claim is defensible but evidence must be acquired; or CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE, the claim cannot survive the standard and should be rewritten or removed. For every HIGH-REGULATORY and CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE: produce a revised compliant version that preserves the brand's intent without the regulatory exposure. Output: a claims-proof map with claim, risk, status, and revised version per row.

props.command:
claude "greenwashing risk check — ECGT + FTC:
  Claims to audit:
  [LIST SUSTAINABILITY / ETHICS CLAIMS]
  For each claim:
  1. Risk: HIGH-REGULATORY (ECGT banned category or
     FTC presumptively deceptive) | MEDIUM | LOW
     Flag banned categories: [ECGT-RISK]
  2. Status: HAVE (substantiation exists) |
     NEED (defensible, evidence required) |
     CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE (rewrite or remove)
  3. For HIGH-REGULATORY / CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE:
     revised compliant version preserving brand intent
  Output: claims-proof map table +
    count of HIGH-REGULATORY before/after revisions"

beats.role_note: ClaudeCodeBeat — actual claims checker script; trimmed to what teaches
beats.narration_text: Read it. The ECGT trigger list is the operational core — the banned words are enumerated, and any claim containing them without a substantiation signal fires the flag. The offset detection looks for offset, carbon credit, carbon neutral via, and net zero via — the language that typically precedes an offset-based claim. The status logic is the second layer: the model cannot know whether the evidence actually exists, so it returns NEED as the default for claims that are defensible but unverified, and CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE for claims whose structural type is banned. That is the correct epistemic position: the tool identifies the exposure, the brand team resolves the evidence.

props.title: greenwash_check.py
props.code:
# greenwash_check.py  —  Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

ECGT_GENERIC = [
    'eco-friendly', 'green', 'sustainable', 'environmentally responsible',
    'nature-friendly', 'climate-smart', 'planet-friendly', 'earth-friendly'
]
OFFSET_SIGNALS = ['offset', 'carbon credit', 'carbon neutral via', 'net zero via']

SYSTEM = f"""You are a greenwashing compliance auditor.
ECGT Directive applies 27 September 2026.
Banned categories:
  1. Generic green claims without substantiation:
     {ECGT_GENERIC}
     → flag [ECGT-RISK] HIGH-REGULATORY
  2. Offset-based climate neutral/net zero at product level:
     signals: {OFFSET_SIGNALS}
     → flag [ECGT-RISK] HIGH-REGULATORY
FTC Green Guides (2012): deceptive claims → HIGH-REGULATORY
For each claim:
  Risk: HIGH-REGULATORY | MEDIUM | LOW
  Status: HAVE | NEED | CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE
  Revised: compliant version for HIGH + CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE
Do NOT invent certifications or evidence.
Do NOT apply ECGT to corporate-level claims — product-level only.
Output: claims-proof map table + summary count."""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
result = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7", max_tokens=2048,
    system=SYSTEM,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sys.stdin.read()}]
)
print(result.content[0].text)
props.sparkLine: Ship the proof, not the claim.

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — claims table with HIGH-REGULATORY rows highlighted
beats.narration_text: Run it on five claims from a consumer brand: eco-friendly packaging — HIGH-REGULATORY, CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE, generic green, [ECGT-RISK]. Carbon neutral by 2030 via certified offsets — HIGH-REGULATORY, CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE, offset-based climate neutral, [ECGT-RISK]. Made with sustainable materials — HIGH-REGULATORY, NEED, generic green. We source responsibly — MEDIUM, NEED. Our packaging uses 30% less plastic than our 2022 baseline — LOW, NEED, specific and verifiable. Two of five claims are immediately banned under ECGT. Revised versions print beside each: eco-friendly packaging becomes packaging weight reduced 30% by 2027 — methodology at URL. Carbon neutral via offsets becomes reducing value-chain emissions 40% by 2030 — annual report tracks progress.
beats.visual_intent: screen-rec mp4: five-row claims table printing — two rows highlighted red ([ECGT-RISK] HIGH-REGULATORY); one amber (MEDIUM); two green (LOW/specific); revised compliant versions printing in green below each HIGH-REGULATORY row; summary: '2/5 HIGH-REGULATORY before → 0/5 after revisions'

beats.narration_text: Now push the checker to distinguish corporate-level from product-level claims. ECGT targets product-level claims specifically — a company-wide commitment to reduce emissions is not banned by the same rule that bans a product labeled carbon neutral via offsets. The distinction matters because many brands will retreat entirely from sustainability claims when they need to retreat only from the product-level generic versions. Ask the checker to re-run with the scope flag applied.

props.command:
claude "re-run greenwashing check with scope distinction:
  Classify each claim:
    PRODUCT-LEVEL: claim made on/about specific product
    CORPORATE-LEVEL: company-wide commitment or policy
  Apply ECGT bans to PRODUCT-LEVEL only.
  Apply FTC substantiation standard to both.
  For CORPORATE-LEVEL generic claims:
    risk is FTC/reputational, not ECGT-banned.
  Re-rate each claim with scope label.
  Are any claims incorrectly flagged once scope applied?"

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — scope-annotated table with fewer HIGH-REGULATORY flags
beats.narration_text: Re-run with scope. The corporate-level commitment — we are committed to reducing our environmental footprint — drops from HIGH-REGULATORY to MEDIUM: not an ECGT ban, but still requires FTC substantiation to avoid deceptive trade practice exposure. The product-level eco-friendly and offset-based claims remain HIGH-REGULATORY. The claims-proof map now has a scope column. One of five claims resolved to lower risk when the correct rule was applied. Four need action before September 2026.
beats.visual_intent: Revised claims table: new SCOPE column (PRODUCT/CORPORATE); corporate-level commitment reclassified from HIGH-REGULATORY to MEDIUM; product-level HIGH-REGULATORY unchanged; annotation: 'ECGT targets product claims — corporate commitments face FTC, not ECGT'; before/after count: 2/5 ECGT-banned, 1/5 FTC-medium, 2/5 LOW

beats.narration_text: The ECGT Directive is not a proposal — it applies from 27 September 2026, and the generic green claim vocabulary that has been standard brand copy for a decade is specifically banned. The offset-based carbon neutral claim is also specifically banned at the product level. The correction is almost always the same: replace the generic with the specific, replace the offset with the reduction, and attach the evidence. A claim you cannot substantiate is not a brand claim — it is a liability. The checker finds it before your legal team does.
beats.visual_intent: Summary: ECGT timeline (March 2024 → Sept 2026); two banned categories as red boxes; claims-proof map structure (claim / risk / status / revised); bottom: 'generic → specific; offset → reduction; claim → evidence; ship Sept 2026 with a proof map, not a slogan'

beats.role_note: HANDOFF LAW — prompt READ aloud AND discussed; greeting 'Your turn.'
beats.narration_text: Your turn. Pull every sustainability or ethics claim you have published — marketing site, about page, product packaging, investor materials. Paste them into this prompt with their scope: product-level or corporate-level. Read the HIGH-REGULATORY flags first — those are the ones with legal exposure after September 2026. For each CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE: write the revised version before it ships again. For each NEED: name the specific evidence required and whether you can acquire it in the next 30 days. The claims-proof map is not just a compliance document. It is the only list of sustainability claims your brand is actually entitled to make.

props.command:
claude "greenwashing risk check for my brand:
  Claims: [PASTE ALL SUSTAINABILITY / ETHICS CLAIMS]
  Scope: [PRODUCT-LEVEL or CORPORATE-LEVEL per claim]
  Flag [ECGT-RISK] on PRODUCT-LEVEL generic green
    and offset-based climate neutral claims.
  Rate: HIGH-REGULATORY | MEDIUM | LOW
  Status: HAVE | NEED | CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE
  For HIGH + CANNOT-SUBSTANTIATE:
    revised compliant version preserving intent.
  Output: claims-proof map + count before/after"

beats.narration_text: Build a Greenwashing Claims Risk Checker with Claude. This is Liam, in for Bear — build it with Claude, then take it apart.
